title: Following user outcry, AMD reinstates memory encryption in consumer CPUs
gist: AMD reversed its silent firmware-level removal of Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME/Memory Guard) from consumer Ryzen 9000-series desktop CPUs after user backlash, pledging to reinstate the BIOS option in a July update.

insights.claim: AMD silently removed Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) from consumer Ryzen 9000-series desktop processors via a firmware/BIOS update, then reversed course after user backlash and will reinstate the option in a July BIOS release.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Shows AMD remains responsive to vocal consumer pressure on feature parity; watch for whether silent firmware-driven feature gating recurs as a margin/segmentation lever.
insights.quote: Based on valuable community feedback, we will reinstate this option in an upcoming BIOS release in July.
insights.locator: Now you see it, now you don't

insights.claim: TSME removal required only firmware changes with no silicon modification, meaning continued support was a matter of will rather than hardware necessity.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Confirms feature differentiation between consumer and Pro CPU tiers can be toggled in software, a lever vendors can use for price segmentation.
insights.quote: With no physical changes required to silicon, continued support was largely, if not purely, a matter of will rather than a necessity required by changes to hardware.

insights.claim: Critics speculate AMD removed TSME from cheaper consumer chips to steer customers toward more costly Pro CPUs, though performance/latency cost (relevant to gamers on the 9000 line) is an alternate explanation.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: Upsell-via-feature-gating is a recurring chipmaker tactic; relevant when assessing AMD consumer-vs-Pro margin mix.
insights.locator: The company has yet to explain

insights.claim: AMD's refusal to explain the change reflects a broader two-decade shift in which dominant tech companies have become less willing to acknowledge product changes or admit mistakes as their market power has grown.
insights.speaker: Dan Goodin
insights.so_what: A reputational-risk lens: as platform dominance rises, corporate transparency declines, raising regulatory and PR exposure.
insights.quote: As the companies' power and dominance have mushroomed, their sense of accountability has diminished proportionately.
